coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of few-layered MoS2 modified BiOI with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaowei
Xia, Jiexiang
Zhu, Wenshuai
Di, Jun
Wang, Bin
Yin, Sheng
Chen, Zhigang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Highly efficient few-layered MoS2/BiOI photocatalysts were successfully prepared by one-step solvent thermal process. The photocatalytic performance of MoS2/BiOI was evaluated by using rhodamine B (RhB) as the target pollution to degrade under visible light irradiation. After the introduction of MoS2 nanosheet, the MoS2/BiOI composites showed excellent photocatalytic performance. The optimal content of MoS2 was 0.5wt%, and the composites exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than the pure BiOI and MoS2/BiOI composites with different MoS2 weight ratios. The primary cause of the enhanced photocatalytic activity of MoS2/BiOI was the formation of heterojunction between BiOI and MoS2, which could promote the electron-hole pairs separation ability. The main active species during the photocatalytic process was discussed by the radicals trapping experiments with the addition of different scavengers. Meanwhile, a possible mechanism was proposed to explicate the improvement of photocatalytic activity upon visible light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2
BiOI
Visible light irradiation
Photocatalytic

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of MoS2/BiOI composites; and (b) EDS spectrum of MoS2/BiOI.
XPS spectra of 1wt% MoS2/BiOI composite: (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Mo 3d, (d) O 1s, (e) I 3d, and (f) S 2p.
FT-IR spectra for MoS2/BiOI with different MoS2 contents.
(a) TEM image of MoS2/BiOI; and (b) HR-TEM image of MoS2/BiOI.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of BiOI, MoS2/BiOI and photolysis of RhB under visible light irradiation; (b) Temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in aqueous solution in the presence of 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI (For interpretation of the references to color in text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of pure BiOI, MoS2 and MoS2/BiOI composites.
Transient photocurrent responses for the pure BiOI and 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI sample.
Comparison of photocatalytic activities of 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI composite for the degradation of RhB with or without adding EDTA-2Na and t-Butanol under visible light irradiation.
Proposed photocatalytic charge transfer mechanism in the MoS2/BiOI composites under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of few-layered MoS2 modified BiOI with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly efficient few-layer MoS2/BiOI photocatalysts were successfully synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal process. The MoS2/BiOI exhibited a significant enhanced photocatalytic performance for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation.

Highly efficient few-layered MoS2/BiOI photocatalysts were successfully prepared by one-step solvent thermal process. The photocatalytic performance of MoS2/BiOI was evaluated by using rhodamine B (RhB) as the target pollution to degrade under visible light irradiation. After the introduction of MoS2 nanosheet, the MoS2/BiOI composites showed excellent photocatalytic performance. The optimal content of MoS2 was 0.5wt%, and the composites exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than the pure BiOI and MoS2/BiOI composites with different MoS2 weight ratios. The primary cause of the enhanced photocatalytic activity of MoS2/BiOI was the formation of heterojunction between BiOI and MoS2, which could promote the electron-hole pairs separation ability. The main active species during the photocatalytic process was discussed by the radicals trapping experiments with the addition of different scavengers. Meanwhile, a possible mechanism was proposed to explicate the improvement of photocatalytic activity upon visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysts have attracted much attention for its promising applications in solving environmental purification and energy crisis [1–3]. So far, with the aim to develop highly efficient photocatalysts, numerous efforts have been put into it. However, the practical applications of which are usually limited by the narrow range of light absorption, high recombination of photo generated carriers, and photocorrosion [4]. Accordingly, highly efficient visible light response photocatalysts have become one of the most promising candidates and received considerable scientific interest [5,6].

Recently, much attention has been attracted to two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets due to their unique electronic, layered structure, and optical properties [7]. In these 2D materials, molybdenum disulfide MoS2 as a typical transition metal sulfides with layered structure, has been investigated extensively. The layers of MoS2 are combined by the weak van der waals force, so the bulk is easy to be exfoliated into single layer or few-layered nanosheets with graphene-like structure. In this study, the few-layered MoS2 nanosheets are successfully prepared by a simple solvothermal reaction. The single layer of MoS2 is consist of S-Mo-S layers that the middle layer of Mo atoms is inserted into two layers of S atoms. So far, the thin MoS2 nanosheets has extraordinary applications in catalysis, photoelectric [8] and energy storage [9], due to its outstanding physical and electrical properties. However, the photocatalytic capability of MoS2 nanosheets is limited by its weak conductivity and the lack of active sites. In order to achieve the ideal photocatalytic activity, MoS2 has been coupled with many semiconductors, such as g-C3N4 [10], CdS [11], TiO2 [12], BiVO4 [13] and Cu2O [14], inducing a larger specific surface area, increased active sites and a better electron transport capacity, thus to enhance photocatalytic activity and efficiency. The results indicate that modified MoS2 can act as the co-catalyst to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the body photocatalysts and the few-layered MoS2 exhibit the better performance.

Bismuth oxyhalides BiOX, which X is halogen elements, as a novel photocatalyst, has shown excellent photocatalytic performance owing to its unique layered structure and suitable bandgaps, and is found to be a promising photocatalyst [15]. Compared with BiOBr and BiOCl, BiOI has the best visible light response with the relatively narrow band gap of 1.7–1.9eV, layered structure and strong internal electric field, and has been proved that it is effective for the degradation of RhB and Methyl Orange (MO). It can absorb most of visible light (λ<700nm). Nevertheless, BiOI still suffers from some shortcomings of slow separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the limitation of band structure. In order to solve these limitations, many means are used to modify the BiOI to prolong the charge carriers lifetime, including morphology modulation [16] and coupling with other semiconductors [17]. In view of the fact that the superiority of few-layered MoS2 and BiOI, using an appropriate method to modify BiOI by few-layered MoS2 will further improve its photocatalytic activity, and it will be an ideal semiconductor in the photocatalytic applications.

In this work, the highly efficient few-layered MoS2/BiOI composites are successfully prepared through a simple and convenient method of one-step solvent thermal. The photocatalytic activity is evaluated to degrade RhB under visible light illumination, and it is demonstrated that the 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI composites exhibit an enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the enhancement photocatalytic mechanism of MoS2/BiOI composites based on the trapping experiments are studied in detail.
All reagents are chemical analysis grade and no further purification for experiments.

Synthesis of MoS2: The MoS2 solid is prepared based on the literature [18]. Typically, 1mmol molybdic oxide (MoO3) and 2.5mmol potassium thiocyanate (KSCN) are put into 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture is completely dissolved in 10mL deionized water with magnetic stirring for several hours at room temperature. Then the autoclave is heated up to 180°C for 24h, and then cooled down to room temperature gradually. Finally, the precipitate is centrifuged and washed several times by deionized water and ethanol to remove the remanent reactants, and then the final products are dried in oven for 8h at 50°C.

Synthesis of MoS2/BiOI: Typically, 1mmol ionic liquid [Bmim]I is first dissolved in 20mL of ethylene glycol methyl ether solution with magnetic stirring. 1mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O is put into the above solution and stirred at room temperature until an uniform mixture solution. Then, the certain amount of MoS2 is added into the above mixture solution with stirring. After being stirred, the mixture is added into 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave is then kept at 160°C for 24h. The obtained samples are collected and washed with water and ethanol for several times. Finally, the brick red samples are dried in oven at 60°C. Under the same reaction condition, the preparation of different contents of MoS2 in the MoS2/BiOI composites are 0.1, 0.5, and 1wt%, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are measured on JEOL JEM-2010 microscope to investigate crystallization of nano materials, the morphology of the nanoparticles, and estimate the size of the nanoparticles. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectra is measured in a Bruker D8 diffractometer (λ=1.54Å), using Cu-Kα radiation, and the scanning range is 10°–80°. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectras (EDS) are obtained by a JEOL JSM-7001F. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectras are collected on Nicolet Model Nexus 470 Fourier transform infrared FT-IR instrument, and the samples are dispersing on KBr pellets. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) are recorded on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with the scan range from 200 to 800nm. The photocurrent is measured on CHI 660B electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Company, Shanghai, China). The elements on the surface of MoS2/BiOI composites are studied by X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) on VG MultiLab 2000 system, the monochromatic source is Mg-Kα.

The RhB is selected as target pollution for the degradation, to characterize the photocatalytic activity of BiOI and MoS2/BiOI samples with a visible light illumination. The visible light source is used by a 300W Xe lamp with a 400nm cutoff filter. The temperature of the photocatalytic experiments is retained at 30°C. Firstly, 30mg photocatalyst is added into different Pyrex photocatalytic reactors with 100mL RhB (10mgL−1) solution. Before the light is turned on, the suspension is magnetically stirred for 30min in the dark to achieve absorption-desorption equilibrium. During photoreaction, 3mL suspension is collected at certain time intervals, and then centrifuged under 13,000rpm for 3min. Finally, the absorbance of RhB solution are measured at 553nm by a UV-2450 spectrophotometer, the corresponding concentration is obtained according to the standard curve and then calculate the rate of degradation. The free radicals trapping experiment is carried out by adding t-Butanol and EDTA-2Na to capture OH and h+, respectively, and the method is similar to the photodegradation experiment.

The photocurrents are measured with an electrochemical analyzer in a standard three-electrode system, and the light source is a 500W Xe arc lamp. Platinum (Pt) electrode is used as a counter electrode and the saturated calomel (Ag/AgCl) electrode as a reference electrode. 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH=7.0) is used as the electrolyte. The BiOI and MoS2/BiOI modified indium tin oxide (ITO) (0.5×1cm2) are used as working electrodes. The working electrodes are prepared by the following method. 0.1mg photocatalyst is dispersed in 200μL ethanol and 200μL ethylene glycol (EG), and then the suspension is ultrasonicated for a period of time to achieve uniform mixing. 20μL solution is pipetted and evenly drop-coated onto ITO glass electrode.

The purity and crystallinity of BiOI and MoS2/BiOI are first characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the diffraction peaks of (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (1 0 4) and (2 1 2) for the composites are consistent with the tetragonal phase of BiOI (JCPDS No. 10-0445), with a good crystallinity and the lattice constants of a=3.994Å, c=9.149Å. However, the patterns of MoS2 are not observed in MoS2/BiOI composites because of its low loading amount in the composites. In the XRD analysis, no other miscellaneous peaks are observed, which indicate that the composites are very pure and the crystal structure of BiOI in the MoS2/BiOI composites is not changed after the introduction of MoS2. The EDS pattern (Fig. 1b) indicates the MoS2/BiOI samples contain Bi, O, I, Mo and S elements. It proves that the samples prepared contain Mo and S elements, which can be further confirmed by XPS.

The surface chemical composition and valence states of the 1wt% MoS2/BiOI composites are investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 shows the typical survey scan and high-resolution spectras of Bi 4f, Mo 3d, O 1s, I 3d, and S 2s peaks for the 1wt% MoS2/BiOI composites. Fig. 2a is the survey scan XPS spectrum, from which can see the elements of Bi, O, I, Mo and S in the MoS2/BiOI composites, which is in accordance with the above EDS result. From the high-resolution Bi 4f XPS spectra (Fig. 2b) can be seen two strong absorption peaks at 158.9eV and 164.4eV, which are index to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively. This suggests that the chemical valence state of Bi element in the MoS2/BiOI composites is +3 [19]. Two peaks of 231.7 and 234.7eV (Fig. 2c) are assigned to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively, indicating the feature of Mo4+ in MoS2/BiOI composites [20]. The peaks of O 1s appear at the binding energies of 529.8 and 531eV (Fig. 2d). For the I 3d (Fig. 2e), two typical peaks of I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 are located at 618.8 and 630.4eV, respectively. This is the characteristic peak of I− in the MoS2/BiOI samples. As shown in Fig. 2f, two distinct peaks of S 2p are consistent with that of Bi 4p peaks. However, the existence of Mo indicates the MoS2 has been introduced into the composite successfully. Therefore, the XPS and XRD analyses confirm the coexistence of MoS2 and BiOI in the MoS2/BiOI composites.

In order to explore the bonding and chemical composition of BiOI and MoS2/BiOI composites, the FT-IR spectra is carried out. From Fig. 3 , the band at about 3425cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OH, which is the deformation vibration of absorbed water molecules [21]. For pure BiOI, the band at 498 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching mode of BiO [22]. Compared with pure BiOI, the peak of MoS2/BiOI composites at 498cm−1 is shifted a little with increasing MoS2 content, indicating an interaction between MoS2 nanosheets and BiOI nanosheets and they have been coupled together successfully. In the FT-IR spectra, the absorption peaks of the ionic liquid cannot be observed. It can be considered that the ionic liquid is easy to be removed by washing with alcohol and water from the surface of the MoS2/BiOI samples. The FT-IR results are in accordance with the XRD analysis, and indicate that the MoS2 has been coupled with BiOI successfully.

The structure information and microscopic morphology of MoS2/BiOI composites are examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the typical TEM image of MoS2 with a thin and irregular nanosheet structure is obtained by the method of solvent thermal at 180°C. The ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets are assembled on the surface of BiOI nanosheets and formed a hierarchical structure. Furthermore, an interface between them is observed from the HR-TEM image (Fig. 4b), implying the formation of a heterojunction structure. It can be seen from HR-TEM image that the composites have two different kinds of lattice fringes. One clear lattice spacing with d=0.615nm is in good consistent with the (0 0 2) crystallographic plane of MoS2. Another fringe with d=0.280nm corresponds to the (1 1 0) crystallographic plane of tetragonal BiOI [23]. This result further indicates the coexistence of BiOI and MoS2 in the composites.

On the basis of the above results, the preparation of MoS2/BiOI photocatalysts is successful. In order to explore the photocatalytic performance of MoS2/BiOI composites with different MoS2 content, RhB is chosen as the target organic contaminant for photodegradation reaction under visible light irradiation (λ>400nm). From Fig. 5 a can be seen that the modification of MoS2 can enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiOI. However, too many MoS2 load on the surface of BiOI nanosheets will prevent the absorption of visible light of the BiOI nanosheets, decreasing the photocatalytic activity. For pure BiOI, 63.2% of the RhB is removed after 60min upon visible light irradiation. Compared with pure BiOI, 72.7% and 86.0% of RhB are respectively degraded by 0.1wt% and 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI at the same time, which should be attributed to their different photocatalytic ability. The photocatalytic activity decreases when MoS2 content is 1wt%. This can be attributed to that too many MoS2 cover on the surface of BiOI will limit the efficiency of charge separation and the light absorption of BiOI. Similar results can also be found in the literatures [24]. The 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI materials display the best photocatalytic activity, which has a 23% improvement compared to pure BiOI material after 60min irradiation. Nevertheless, after 100min irradiation, the concentration of RhB has been reduced by about 95.2%. Fig. 5b is the time-resolved spectra of RhB solution over 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI composites. It testifies that during the degradation process, the maximum absorption peaks at 553nm are gradually decreased, and almost disappeared with the irradiation time increasing. Meanwhile, the color changes from amaranth to light pink and then disappeared, indicating the 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI can make the RhB completely decolorization. In the process of photocatalytic degradation, the absorption band is shifted from 553 to 500nm after 100min irradiation, which attributes to the step-by-step process of de-ethyl [25]. The results of photocatalytic degradation indicate that when the BiOI is modified with MoS2, the photocatalytic performance can be improved greatly.

The DRS spectras of BiOI, MoS2, and MoS2/BiOI composites, to detect the optical absorption ability of them, are carried out and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . The absorption edge of pure BiOI is around 670nm, which shows that the BiOI is an immense visible light responsive catalyst. The color of MoS2 nanosheets is dark black, so the MoS2 shows a significant absorption in the range of UV light and visible light. With the increase of the MoS2 content, the color of MoS2/BiOI composites changes to dark gray. After loading MoS2 nanosheets on the surface of BiOI, the optical absorption of the composites in the visible light region is enhanced. Furthermore, the absorption intensity of MoS2/BiOI composites in the range of 640–800nm is strengthened with the amount of MoS2 increased from 0.1wt% to 1wt%. The different colors of the composites lead to the differences of optical adsorption. After MoS2 modified on BiOI samples, the enhanced visible light absorption leads to produce more electron-hole pairs [26], which soon afterwards result in a higher photocatalytic activity.

The transient photocurrent responses of the as-prepared BiOI and MoS2/BiOI are recorded for the periodic on-off. After irradiated by light, the electron on the CB of BiOI and MoS2/BiOI samples transferred to the ITO electrodes, and then photocurrent is generated [27]. The high intensity of photocurrent corresponds to a better separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [28]. As shown in Fig. 7 , when the light is switched on, the photocurrent responses of the working electrodes are sharply increased, which is due to the fast separation of the photoinduced charges. Conversely, when the light is turned off, the photocurrent is very low and maintains its dark current state. It also can be observed that the photocurrent of 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI is higher than that of pure BiOI, which confirms an enhanced separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The result demonstrates that when MoS2 nanosheets are modified on the surface of BiOI, there will be an interfacial electrons transfer between them, thus enhancing the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, extending the lifetime of the electron-hole pairs effectively [29], so as to improve the photocatalytic activity.

As is well known, the main active species for photocatalytic oxidation are hydroxyl radicals (OH) and photogenerated holes (h+) [30]. Therefore, various trapping scavengers are added in 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI samples to detect active species on photocatalytic degradation of RhB upon visible light irradiation. In this study, EDTA-2Na [31] and t-Butanol [32] has been employed as the scavengers for h+ and OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 , in the case of 0.5wt% MoS2/BiOI composites, the RhB is degraded only 15% when EDTA-2Na is added, indicating that the inhibition of the degradation of RhB is significantly by the adding of the hole scavenger. Nevertheless, a weak effect on the photodegradation of RhB by the adding of t-Butanol can be observed, suggesting a small amount of OH is generated. These results confirm that the holes play a key role in the degradation process, while the hydroxyl radical OH has little effect on the photocatalytic process. Hence, during the photodegradation of RhB over MoS2/BiOI photocatalyst, the holes are the principal active species, and OH radicals play a bit role in that.

Based on the above results, a possible mechanism for the enhancement of photocatalytic properties of MoS2/BiOI composites is proposed (Fig. 9 ). The minimum position of conduction band (CB) and the maximum position of valence band (VB) of BiOI and MoS2 are estimated by using the empirical formulae: EVB =X−Ee +0.5Eg and ECB =EVB −Eg [33], where X is the absolute electro negativity, Ee is a constant of the free electrons energy with respect to the hydrogen scale (≈4.5eV), and Eg is the forbidden bandwidth of semiconductors. On the basis of UV–vis spectra, the band gap energy of BiOI nanosheets is 1.82eV. The band gap energy of MoS2 nanosheets is 1.90eV [23]. Thereupon then, the calculated EVB and ECB of BiOI are 2.35 and 0.53eV, respectively. The EVB and ECB of MoS2 are 1.81 and −0.09eV, respectively. Under visible light irradiation (λ>400nm), the photogenerated electrons on the VB are excited and transferred to the corresponding CB, and the holes are left on the valence band of BiOI and MoS2. Because of the CB potential of the MoS2 is more negative than that of BiOI, the photoinduced electrons on the CB of the MoS2 can easily transfer to the CB of BiOI through the interface. At the same time, the VB position of the MoS2 is more negative than that of the BiOI, so the holes can migrate from BiOI to the MoS2, thus the efficient separation of photogenerated carrier occurs. Whereas the VB potentials of MoS2 (+1.81eV) and BiOI (+2.35eV) are less positive than E0(OH/OH−) and E0(OH/H2O), which are +2.38eV (vs NHE) and +2.72eV (vs NHE), respectively, resulting in the holes oxidation of adsorbed OH− and H2O cannot generate OH radicals [34,35]. That is to say, the photogenerated holes is directly oxidized the absorbed pollutant molecules to produce the final photodegradation products.

In summary, the few-layered MoS2/BiOI photocatalyst was synthesized via a simple and convenient solvothermal process. In comparison with pure BiOI, the MoS2/BiOI composites exhibited an advanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The optimum content of MoS2 was 0.5wt% to the MoS2/BiOI composites, which had the best photocatalytic activity, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was 95.2% after 100min irradiation. The free radical trapping experiments showed that the main active specie was holes, which played an important role in the process of the degradation of RhB. The MoS2/BiOI composites, possessed a high visible light photocatalytic activity, might be a promising candidate photocatalyst applied in the degradation of harmful organic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), the Doctoral Innovation Fund of Jiangsu Province (KYZZ16_0340) and the Science and Technology support program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018).
